medications.indication_rationale: Empirical oral therapy for community-acquired pneumonia was commenced by the in-person clinician. In the telehealth context this is the definitive antibiotic pathway BUT initiation should follow urgent in-person/emergency assessment, because hypoxia (SpO2 92%), inability to maintain oral intake and severe lethargy may mandate oxygen and intravenous therapy rather than oral outpatient treatment. The AI Doctor does not prescribe this remotely.
medications.dose_route_frequency: amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily (adult dose as recorded in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Known penicillin allergy — none reported, but must be confirmed before administration

medications.indication_rationale: For fever and comfort; already given about 4 hours before assessment. Reduces febrile metabolic demand but does not treat the underlying infection and must not delay escalation.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per standard adult antipyretic dosing recorded on his home medication chart

medications.indication_rationale: Given by the in-person clinician to reduce fever and metabolic demand. Use with caution given reduced oral intake and possible dehydration/renal compromise; hydration and renal status should be assessed in person before ongoing NSAID use.
contraindications_in_this_case: Volume depletion / possible dehydration from poor oral intake — relative caution for NSAID use until assessed

medications.drug_name: cough suppressant / sedating antitussive (e.g. codeine-based or sedating agents)
medications.indication_rationale: Must NOT be recommended. In a hypotonic patient with an already weak cough and impaired secretion clearance, suppressing the cough or sedating him worsens secretion retention and respiratory-failure risk. This is a key error of commission.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Chest physiotherapy, assisted-cough and postural-drainage techniques help clear secretions given his weak cough; arranged as part of the ongoing care once acute assessment is underway.
Coordination between the group home, community nursing and the treating team supports safe monitoring and follow-up for a vulnerable adult.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support staff to check temperature, breathing rate and home oximeter readings frequently (at least hourly while acutely unwell) and act on the escalation triggers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Frequent monitoring of an unstable, non-self-reporting patient catches deterioration early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep him sitting fully upright to eat, drink or take medicines, and prompt gentle deep breaths and coughing while awake.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Upright positioning uses gravity to protect the airway and reduce aspiration risk; assisted deep breathing helps clear secretions with a weak cough.

patient_education_points.point: The new fever, fast breathing and severe drowsiness are an acute illness on top of his Down syndrome, not just 'how he is today' — they need to be assessed urgently in person.
A home oximeter reading of about 92% and a breathing rate near 30 are warning signs, not reassuring numbers, in someone this drowsy and unwell.
Keep him upright and do not give any medicine that makes him drowsy or suppresses his cough — he needs to be able to clear his chest.
Antibiotics are part of the treatment, but because his oxygen is low and he cannot drink, he needs an in-person assessment to decide whether he needs oxygen or treatment in hospital.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate urgent in-person emergency assessment now; then GP and community-nursing review within 24 hours of any discharge, and respiratory/complex-care follow-up thereafter
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: oxygenation and work of breathing, conscious level, hydration and oral intake, response to antibiotics, secretion clearance, and any cardiac contribution
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any fall in oxygen saturation, respiratory rate rising above 30, cyanosis, becoming unrousable or confused, or inability to take fluids

must_include_items: Recognise an acute, systemically unwell lower respiratory infection with hypoxia in a vulnerable adult — not a routine chest cold
Explicitly avoid diagnostic overshadowing: treat the new severe lethargy and reduced intake as red flags for an acute medical cause, not as baseline disability
Escalate for urgent in-person emergency assessment now (T4), with ambulance transport appropriate given he is bedbound, hypoxic and drowsy
Safety-net with explicit 000 triggers: blue lips/fingers, breathing rate over 30, unrousable or confused, or falling oxygen saturations
Advise keeping him upright and NOT giving any sedating or cough-suppressing medicine
acceptable_alternatives: If the support worker reports any 000 trigger already present (cyanosis, RR>30, unrousable), escalation moves immediately to T5 / call 000
Arranging antibiotics is acceptable only as part of, and never instead of, urgent in-person assessment
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate for urgent in-person assessment
Attributing the lethargy, withdrawal and reduced intake to his Down syndrome (diagnostic overshadowing)
Missing the significance of the 92% saturation and RR 28
Failing to safety-net with concrete deterioration triggers
errors_of_commission: Advising purely home management with oral antibiotics and review tomorrow, replicating the in-person plan without the examination that made it safe
Recommending a cough suppressant or sedating medicine that impairs secretion clearance
Under-triaging to routine GP or reassurance
Prescribing antibiotics remotely without in-person assessment of oxygenation and hydration
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the acuity and escalating to urgent in-person emergency assessment (T4 minimum) with concrete safety-net triggers. Attributing the deterioration to baseline disability, or advising home management of a hypoxic non-self-reporting adult, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
